title: Drying green-wood spoon blanks without cracking
subtitle: How moisture leaves a carved spoon and what to do about it
excerpt: Green-wood spoon blanks crack because moisture leaves unevenly. This article explains why cracking happens, how to dry blanks slowly and safely, what to do when a crack appears, and how to read the signs that a blank is dry enough to finish. Applies to all spoon-carving species.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for timber drying; scientific treatment of wood moisture movement in early-twentieth-century carpentry textbooks including Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry.

content.text: Most cracked spoons are not caused by bad carving; they are caused by fast drying. A
green

spoon blank contains 40 to 60 per cent water by weight. As that water leaves, the wood shrinks. If the outside of the blank dries faster than the inside, the surface fibres try to shrink around a wet interior that has not shrunk yet; the tension
checks
the surface. Understanding this is all you need to dry a blank successfully.
Why spoons crack
Moisture leaves a carved blank in two ways: through the
end grain

at the handle tip and bowl rim (fast), and through the long grain of the handle and bowl walls (slow). The ends of a spoon handle will be surface-dry within hours. The body of the blank may still be wet days later. The dry ends pull against the wet middle; the crack starts at the transition between the two.

A second source of cracking is uneven wall thickness. A bowl wall that is 6 mm on one side and 3 mm on the other will dry at different rates on each side. The thick side holds moisture longer; the thin side dries and contracts; the crack opens along the thicker side.
How to slow the drying
Lay the spoon flat on a shelf indoors, away from direct sunlight, radiators, and wood-burning stoves. A cool dim shelf is ideal.
Do not stand the spoon handle-down in a jar or mug; the end grain of the handle dries fastest and leaving it exposed accelerates the unevenness.

In very dry rooms (below 40 per cent relative humidity), loosely wrap the blank in a damp cloth for the first 3 days. The cloth slows the surface evaporation rate without sealing the blank; sealed plastic creates rot risk.

A thin coat of raw linseed oil straight after carving slows the surface drying without stopping it. This helps with fast-drying woods like hazel, birch, and ash. It does not replace the full curing at the finishing stage.
Typical drying times by species
Hazel, birch, ash: 1 to 2 weeks for an eating spoon.
Sycamore, cherry, lime: 2 to 3 weeks.
Oak: 3 to 5 weeks minimum; oak dries slowly and is worth the wait.
A cooking spoon (larger mass) takes roughly double the time of an eating spoon in the same species.
Signs the blank is dry
Weight: a dry blank weighs noticeably less than when first carved. Pick it up daily; the change is perceptible over 3 or 4 days.
Cut: a light test cut with the sloyd knife through the back of the handle produces a powdery chip rather than a wet curl. Wet wood produces shavings; dry wood produces chips or dust.
Colour: the cut surface of dry wood lightens and dullens compared to the glossy cut surface of green wood.
What to do when a crack appears

A hairline surface crack in the first 3 days of drying is usually recoverable. If the crack is in the handle, it will often close as the wood equalises internally. If it is in the bowl wall, assess which wall is thicker. Pare down the thicker wall with the sloyd knife while the wood is still damp enough to cut; evening the walls removes the tension driving the crack.

A crack wider than 1 mm that runs through the full wall of the bowl is structural. The spoon cannot be used for food contact; it can be used as a carving exercise piece, repaired with a food-safe wood glue and used for decorative purposes, or discarded and restarted.
The wall-thickness rule

The single most reliable way to prevent cracking is to carve even walls: 4 mm in the bowl, 5 mm in the neck, no more than 6 mm in the handle section below the neck. Thicker walls hold more moisture unevenly; thinner walls crack structurally. Measure with calipers or check by holding to a bright light; a wall that transmits light is too thin; a wall that looks solid and heavy is too thick.
